title: Trading Against the Random Expiration of Private Information: A Natural Experiment
abstract: For years, the Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) accidentally distributed securities disclosures to some investors before the public. We exploit this setting, which is unique because the delay until public disclosure was exogenous and the private information window was well defined, to study informed trading with a random stopping time. Trading intensity and the pace at which prices incorporate information decrease with the expected delay until public release, but the relation between trading intensity and time elapsed varies with traders' learning process. Noise trading and relative information advantage play similar roles as in standard microstructure theories assuming a fixed time window.

article.introduction:
For more than two decades,the Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) has provided investors with access to securities filings containing market-moving information through its Electronic Data Gathering, Analysis, and Retrieval (EDGAR) system, which is available through the SEC's website. And for years, unbeknownst to lawmakers and the public, a small group of private investors were inadvertently given access to these filings before they were widely released via EDGAR—a government contractor operating a platform known as the Public Dissemination Service, or PDS, distributed SEC filings to a small number of paying subscribers moments before they reached the public. In October 2014, theWall Street Journalexposed this issue.1In response, members of Congress demanded that the SEC examine the problem. Two months later, SEC Chairman Mary Jo White pledged to Congress that the Commission would quickly eliminate PDS subscribers' advantage.2
There is no evidence that either the SEC or the government contractor acted opportunistically. Instead, the issue appears to have been due to a lack of engineering coordination between the public website and the PDS feed (see SectionI.A). Nevertheless, the episode provides a rare lab-like setting for studying how speculators trade on, and the stock market processes, private information that expires at a random time. Moreover, because the informed traders could form expectations about the length of the delay based on factors that were exogenous to traders' behavior, we are able to identify the causal impact of the expected delay on trading patterns.
More specifically, this setting contains two features that are typically not available to researchers. First, we can observe both the arrival time and the content of private information. While the theoretical literature (pioneered by Glosten and Milgrom (1985) and Kyle (1985)) has developed a standard framework for how securities prices incorporate private information through the work of informed traders, relatively few studies test these theories based on private information with well-identified content and timing. This is because private information is, by definition, not public knowledge and thus neither the timing of its arrival nor its content are generally observable by econometricians.3In our setting, we observe both the exact time when a small group of investors receive the information (the time when a filing reaches PDS subscribers) and the exact content of the information (the filing) that the investors receive.
Second, we can measure the length of time that the informed traders have the information before a filing reaches the public. As explained in detail below, the duration of the “private window” in our setting varies randomly due to technical limitations of the EDGAR system. Our data confirm that the only factor that explains a significant portion of the variance in the length of the delay is the time of day, presumably because it is correlated with the volume of traffic on the servers. Critically, the delay was beyond the control of both the filers and the speculators, and is not correlated with the variables of interest. This unique source of exogenous variation allows us to draw causal inferences about speculators as well as show how market prices incorporate private information that is based on the expected length of the delay.
To provide a unified framework for our empirical tests, we develop a model of informed trading with a random stopping time but a known hazard rate from an exponential distribution. The model is closely related to Caldentey and Stacchetti (2010), with modifications made to fit the institutional features of our setting. In Caldentey and Stacchetti (2010), an insider has perfect knowledge of the real-time value of the security, which follows a diffusion process. In our model, in contrast, features an insider who attempts to learn the fixed liquidation value over time. This feature fits our setting more closely because the fundamental value of a security is unlikely to change during an interval of a few minutes and insiders may keep refining their estimation of the true value by processing the leaked filings, knowing that the private information could become public at a random time over which the insiders can form an expectation.
We next develop several empirical measures that closely track the parameters in our model. Expected delay is the predicted delay, over which time (hour) of the day is the primary determinant. Trading intensity is captured by both total trades and directional trades (buying or selling in the “right” direction based on the dollar value and the trade count). Consistent with the model's predictions, we find that when the expected delay is longer, insiders trade less, in terms of both the dollar value of trades and the number of trades. In addition, when the expected delay is longer, insiders take more time to submit the first trade after the leakage. Moreover, both the information content and the ease of information processing of the different types of filings affect trading speed and intensity. Schedule 13D filings,4which tend to generate more sizable abnormal returns upon public announcement (as compared to, e.g., Form 4 filings) and are relatively straightforward to process (as compared to, e.g., Form 8-K filings), induce the fastest and most intense trading during the private window.
Finally, we take advantage of this unique setting to test the standard insider trading models. Consistent with Kyle (1985), we find that speculators trade more aggressively when the value-price divergence is larger, when the filing entails high information content (measured ex ante or ex post), and when the market is deeper (measured ex ante or in real time). We also find that trading via limit orders is preferred to trading via market orders when there is more time (i.e., when the expected delay is longer) and when market-wide trading volume is high and volatility is low. Although insiders attempt to smooth out the price impact of their trading, we find that information disseminates faster among large-cap, high-turnover stocks and when the overall market experiences more trading activity.
Our study contributes to the vast literature on information transmission and asset pricing (for comprehensive surveys, see Easley and O'Hara (2003), Biais, Glosten, and Spatt (2005), and Tetlock (2014)) by making typically unobservable private information the subject of our empirical tests. Financial markets play a fundamental role in the aggregation of information and the allocation of investment capital (Bond, Edmans, and Goldstein (2012)). Our study offers insights into the microstructure of how the stock market performs these roles.
Importantly, our study complements, but is distinct from, several recent papers that study the relationship between the distribution of information to investors through the Internet and price discovery (Bauguess, Cooney, and Hanley (2013), Drake, Roulstone, and Thornock (2015), Loughran and McDonald (2017)). Most notably, the subject of our study is private—rather than public—information. Two other recent working papers examine market responses to private information before its public release (Hu, Pan, and Wang (2017) and Rogers, Skinner, and Zechman (2017)). The latter was motivated by the same episode and covers a larger sample, but it limits attention to only one type of SEC filing (Form 4) and focuses on the abnormal returns associated with the leakage. Moreover, unlike our study, these papers do not develop or test microstructure theories using trade-level data. In addition, none of the studies above incorporates or explores the random variation in the duration of the private-information window.
Recent work most closely related to our study is Collin-Dufresne and Fos (2015) and Koudijs (2015a,2015b), both of whom adopt ingenious research designs to identify the path of private information and its effect on stock prices. Our design differs from that of Collin-Dufresne and Fos (2015) in that the private information in our paper relates to the financial and operational—that is, the fundamental—condition of the firm itself. In contrast, the private information studied in Collin-Dufresne and Fos (2015) pertains to the trader's intention to intervene. As a result, both the creation and the duration of the private information are endogenous to the informed trader, which imply different strategic behaviors in trading on private information as theoretically modeled by Back et al. (2018).5
Our study also complements work by Koudijs (2015a,2015b), who uses boats carrying mail between London and Amsterdam during the 18th century as the conduit for information flow to the stock exchanges in these cities to uncover the strategic behavior of informed traders. In that setting, the exogenous variation in expected delay was driven by weather conditions. Today, electronic information dissemination is the 21st-century equivalent of boats carrying news. In both settings, however, information dissemination slows down as expected delays increase. The two papers therefore support each other. The novel contribution of our study lies in that we observe both the content of the private information as well as resulting trading patterns at the individual securities level at a high frequency (seconds). We are thus able to study how information reaches and is processed by markets via trades at a level that is infeasible using historical data. We believe that studying similar episodes from these two distant eras offers new and complementary insights on the fundamentals of informed trading as well as on how stock markets have evolved.
The rest of the paper is organized as follows. SectionIintroduces the institutional background and the construction as well as the overview of the sample data that build on the accidental early leakage of SEC filings prior to October 2014. SectionIIsets up a parsimonious theory model that derives both the optimal strategy of a speculator whose private information expires at a random stopping time, and the resulting impact on the market microstructure. SectionIIIpresents empirical tests of the model predictions based on the microstructure trading and pricing data associated with the SEC filings during the “private window” from the early leakage to public disclosure. SectionIVconcludes.
article.I. Institutional Background, Sample Data:
U.S. securities laws impose rigid rules on publicly traded companies, requiring that these firms disclose material information about themselves to investors in a timely manner. These disclosures are typically provided in the form of securities filings submitted to the SEC. As a result, the SEC has become a central repository for information that moves markets. Specifically, the SEC's EDGAR system is the central portal through which firms can disclose, and investors can retrieve, new information that can potentially affect a firm's fundamental value.
Prior to the discovery of the discrepancy studied here, when a company electronically submitted a securities filing to the SEC, the filing was distributed to three locations. The first is the SEC's file transfer protocol (FTP) server. The FTP system is simply a repository of files that can be downloaded on demand once located. Each filer has its own “directory,” and new filings are deposited there immediately. But with tens of thousands of filers (directories), it was impossible to know where the next filing would appear, and the effort required to scrape all directories would likely have defeated the lead in time the FTP server provided over the EDGAR website. The PDS system solved this problem as it was intentionally engineered to rapidly “push” new filings to subscribers in real time.
Upon the submission of a new filing, the private contractor operating the PDS service distributed the filing to a small group of about 40 subscribers at a cost of approximately $15,000 per year.6The filing was then uploaded to the SEC's EDGAR system, which is available to the investing public through the SEC's website. While in theory these systems should have operated simultaneously, technical limitations led to random delays between the time when filings reached the SEC's FTP server, the time when they were distributed to PDS subscribers, and the time when they were made available to the public on EDGAR. In particular, the length of the delay before filings reached the EDGAR website over the period we study ranged from a few seconds to as long as several minutes.
The principal data, which contain the exact time stamps of filings arriving at different portals, come from Jackson and Mitts (2014)7and cover the period June 25 to October 15, 2014. Our data for delivery to PDS subscribers begin on August 1, 2014, and continue through October 15, 2014. Using the overlapping part of the sample, we find that the FTP and PDS time stamps were almost identical, featuring differences of no more than a few seconds and a median absolute difference of one second. As such, we use the FTP time stamp as a proxy for the time advantage of the “early informed” in an effort to preserve a larger sample.
The closeness of the two time stamps (FTP versus PDS) is not a coincidence but rather is an outcome of the infrastructure of the system. Neither FTP nor PDS involves an “intermediary” (a public website). On the other hand, the EDGAR website is the only place where the general public can find new securities filings (without knowing in advance which firms have filed), but because the website contains an entire user interface, it is systematically slower. When a high number of filings are submitted within a very short time, the exact order in which they appear online is as good as random from the standpoint of each user. Even if some issuers choose the time of day when they upload their filings—which, as we explained below, is correlated with the average delay—the actual delay is beyond the control of the speculators, who are the key players in our analyses. Internet Appendix SectionIII.Aprovides more technical details.8
Our sample period ends immediately before the public revelation of the PDS-public time gap, which shrank precipitously afterwards (though it did not completely disappear). Figure1shows the percentage of filings released to PDS before EDGAR from August 2014 to May 2015 using a “heat map” that monitors the situation in real time during that period.
PDS-EDGAR delay heat map: August 2014 to May 2015. This heat map monitors the percentage of filings released to PDS before EDGAR from August 2014 to May 2015. The continuous monitoring was maintained by two of the authors (Jackson and Mitts). [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]
Our study builds on the premise that some of the early-informed traded to their advantage. Anecdotal evidence suggests that a few hedge funds were among the PDS subscribers, including the fact that Rogers, Skinner, and Zechman (2017) obtained their data from an anonymous hedge fund. Moreover, both Jackson and Mitts (2014) and Rogers, Skinner, and Zechman (2017) document abnormal returns prior to the public release of filings, for which informed trading is the most likely cause. We further strengthen this premise in SectionI.Cby documenting abnormal trading volume at the second-by-second level during the private window. Throughout our analysis of this unique setting, we also maintain the standard assumption in the insider trading literature that the early-informed trade monopolistically rather than competitively. In reality, including our setting, it is reasonable to expect that at least a few players will have access to private information and will trade with the knowledge that they are not the sole recipients of the information. Holden and Subrahmanyam (1992) show that informed trades become more aggressive quickly even with modest competition.
We believe that the competition does not lead to near-instant revelation of private information for at least two reasons. First, the group of early-informed investors is expected to be small relative to the number of participants in the market. The PDS service had about 40 paying subscribers during this period. Anecdotal evidence suggests that a few of these PDS subscribers were wire services,9but the end-users of the wire services likely did not have real-time access to the original filings. These wire services disseminate brief summaries of the original filing quickly, but by the time these services write and post news articles, the filings are typically available on the EDGAR website. Alternatively, using the FTP server to detect unexpected filings is technically difficult. An interested investor would be required to navigate, without any delay, to the server directory associated with a particular firm. Because it is difficult to know ex ante which firms will file unexpected or unscheduled filings, which constitute about 95% of our sample, we think that the number of investors who had early access to filings via FTP was relatively small.10Finally, the few informed traders in our setting were clearly aware of both the circumstance that afforded them an advantage and size of the informed group (even though they did not necessarily know the identities of the other members). In what is essentially a repeated game, they may also have had an incentive to trade less aggressively as the models of competition suggest.
Second, in our model the insiders learn about the true value gradually, over time. Their estimates of the true value likely differ at any given point, even if based on the same leaked filing. Therefore, although Holden and Subrahmanyam (1992) predict that “common private information” will be incorporated into prices very quickly with competition, the informed trading in our setting will be more prolonged given potentially different interpretations of the information embedded in the filings.
To set the stage for sample construction, we define the following key points in the timeline of events with respect to each individual filing:
t1: The PDS/FTP time stamp, or the time when the “early informed” receive the information.
t2: The EDGAR time stamp, or the time when the filing becomes public. Accordingly,(t2−t1)is the length of the “private window,” that is the length of time over which the information remains private. We refer to this time lag asDelay.
We note that companies occasionally issue press releases prior to formal SEC filings. Importantly, this would make it less likely to find any results, as the incremental information content of some filings may have already been reduced by the time the filing reaches PDS subscribers. Notwithstanding, for robustness we use the Dow Jones Newswires database to examine whether newswires feature the filing firms with time stamps ahead of the public release on EDGAR on the same day. We find this to be true for less than 0.1% of the filings. Settingt2to be the latter of the newswire and EDGAR time stamps does not change our results in any noticeable way, as we discuss in Internet Appendix SectionIII.C.
t3: A time proxy for the end of the period(t2,t3)over which public investors are given adequate opportunity to trade on the information revealed by the SEC filing.
We sett3to bet1+15minutes, a time that is independent of the delay(t2−t1)and incorporates over 10 minutes during which the information could be publicly digested. The full information contained in the filing may or may not be fully revealed in price byt3. For example, Huang, Tan, and Wermers (2016) show that the full day after a news release is the day during which institutional investors exhibit significant abnormal trading volume. However, because privately informed status is not necessary for trading on information aftert2, the firm's stock price shortly after public release, that is, att3, is a plausible proxy for the relevant “terminal value” for speculators. This is also consistent with the view of Foucault, Hombert, and Rosu (2016) that “fast traders” do not care about the long-term effects of information, as they trade only against the market reaction to the public release of news.
Our entire data set includes 101,555 securities disclosures that public companies electronically filed with the SEC from June 25 to October 15, 2014, with the exception of July 15, 2014, as technical difficulties with connecting to the SEC's systems prevented us from collecting data on that day. We limit our sample to only those filings made by publicly traded firms by linking a filer's Central Index Key (CIK) identifier and its exchange ticker to its entry in CRSP/Compustat. In addition, we remove filings that arrived on the EDGAR website prior to the FTP server, that is, when there is no private window, which occurred occasionally due to the random nature of the delay. These filters reduce our sample to 42,619 filings, which constitute our “Initial Sample.”
The distribution ofDelay, ort2−t1, is strongly skewed to the right in the Initial Sample. The median delay is 26 seconds, with an interquartile range of 7 to 172 seconds. We impose the additional filter for sample inclusion thatDelaymust not exceed 466 seconds, or the 90th percentile value. Trimming these outliers not only limits the influence of extreme observations but also takes into account the possibility that the monitoring script may have periodically “hung” due to server overload, introducing erroneous and implausible delays into the data. We believe that the top decile reflects a conservative estimate of delays that are likely to be erroneous, but our results are robust to using a reasonable lower cutoff. Such trimming leaves us with a “Trimmed Sample” of 38,352 filings, which we refer to broadly as “our sample.” The exact number of observations in the regressions is slightly lower and varies depending on both the matching of the filings to the Trade and Quote (TAQ) database and individual variable availability.
Of our sample filings 18% are Form 8-K filings (involving disclosure of material corporate events), 51% Form 4 filings (disclosure of insiders' trades in the company's stock; current U.S. securities law requires that insiders provide such disclosure within 48 hours of each trade), 1% Schedule 13D filings (disclosure of beneficial ownership of 5% or more; current law requires that this Schedule be filed within 10 days after the investor crosses the 5% threshold), and 30% other miscellaneous types (such as 10-Q filings). A great majority (over 95%) of the filings in this sample are nonscheduled, that is, are contingent on unanticipated events rather than a predetermined filing date. This is not surprising because scheduled filings, such as Forms 10-Q and 10-K, are relatively few per year/quarter while the number of nonscheduled filings is potentially unlimited. Thus, investors could not have anticipated the arrival of the overwhelming majority of the filings we study.
The key variable in our analyses isDelay, the time difference between the private and public release of filings. While it is largely random in the sense that the exact delay is out of the control of both the informed traders and the issuers, its aggregate statistics appear to be affected by thetotalvolume of submissions to, and the traffic on, the SEC's EDGAR servers at a given point in time. As a result, theexpecteddelayshows a distinct pattern in relation to the time of day of the filing. This pattern can be seen in Figure2, which plots the average delay(t2−t1)by hourly bins from 6:00 a.m. to 11:00 p.m. as well as the intensity of filings throughout the day. The average delay reaches its peak (over 150 seconds) just after 4:00 p.m. EST, the close of the trading day on the formal market—presumably when there is high filing traffic, which leads to delays in the transmission of filings to the public EDGAR servers.
Average FTP-public delay and filing intensity throughout the trading day. On the left vertical axis, this figure shows the average delay in the public release of all filings with the SEC during the period June 25, 2014, to October 15, 2014 (excluding July 15, 2014) by hourly intervals in the Trimmed Sample. The delay is calculated as the difference in time, in seconds, between the SEC's file transfer protocol (FTP) time stamp for the filing—a proxy for the actual time that filings reach the subscribers of the Public Dissemination Service (PDS)—and the SEC's Electronic Data Gathering, Analysis, and Retrieval System (EDGAR) time stamp (the actual time when filings are released to the public). On the right vertical axis, the figure shows the percentage of all filings in the Trimmed Sample that occur during each hour. The Trimmed Sample contains all filings censored at the 90thpercentile of delay, or 466 seconds, as longer delays could be due to measurement errors resulting from “server hang.” [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]
Trades take place before the market opens at 9:30 a.m. and continue after the close of the market at 4:00 p.m. These after-hours trades, including preopen (8:00–9:30 a.m.) and postclose (4:00–6:30 p.m.), are not a recent phenomenon; trades have been executed regularly during these hours on electronic communications networks (ECNs) for decades. For example, Barclay and Hendershott (2003) document that, among the 250 highest volume stocks on the NASDAQ exchange in 2000, about 2.5% of the trading volume occurs preopen and another 5.5% postclose. After-hours trades account for 9.6% of the trades in our sample.
TableI, Panel A, reports summary statistics for filing and security characteristics. The main variables capturing the leakage events are length of delay, abnormal return during the private time window, and bid-ask spread just prior tot1(the beginning of the private time window). We further summarize statistics for the firm-level variablesMarketcapitalization, the market capitalization of the issuer at the end of June 2014,Idiocyncraticvol, the annualized idiosyncratic volatility of the stock calculated as the residual volatility from the Fama and French (1992) three-factor model, andShareturnover, the daily average share turnover rate. The last two variables are constructed using daily data over the 252 trading days prior to June 30, 2014, and are commonly adopted proxies for both the ex ante informativeness of signals and the level of noise trading from standard microstructure models.
Finally, we report summary statistics for three variables that control for real-time general market conditions. The market trading volume (in billions of dollars) and volatility (at the minute-level frequency, in basis points) of SPY, the exchange traded fund (ETF) that tracks the S&P 500 and the most actively traded ETF that tracks a major index, are recorded during the hour to whicht1belongs. The number of SEC filings uploaded during the 20-minute interval prior tot1captures both the quantity of new information arrival in the market and the level of traffic on the SEC server.
Panel B reports summary statistics for|AR(t1,t3)|, a proxy for the potential informativeness of the filings, by filing type for both the Trimmed Sample and a subsample requiring a nonzero price change fromt1tot3. In terms of sample means, Form 8-K filings induce the largest price changes, and Form 4 filings the least. For the Trimmed Sample, the average Form 8-K generates an absolute abnormal return of 0.62%, 15 minutes after the early release. By contrast, the same number for a Form 4 filing is 0.17%. The standard deviation of abnormal returns follows the same pattern. The order of these statistics is preserved if we focus on the subsample with nonzero price changes during[t1,t3]. Therefore, Form 8-K (Form 4) provides the most (least) value for trading by early-informed speculators. The differences between all other form types and Form 4 are statistically significant at the 1% level.
Since we study informed trading associated with SEC filing leakage, it is important to establish that there was unusually high trading volume during the private windows. To this end, we plot the number of trades and dollar value of trades per second, starting from the first second just after the private release of filings, averaged over all filings in the Trimmed Sample with kernel smoothing. Figure3plots the two series with 95% confidence intervals. The “normal” level of trades, or the benchmark, is set to the average from a “placebo sample”: Because the SEC largely fixed the leakage in February 2015 (see Figure1), the period thereafter provides a valuable out-of-sample context to gauge the “counterfactual” trade patterns under the null of no leakage. We generate the placebo sample by examining all EDGAR filings in March 2015 by publicly traded companies. For each placebo filing, we randomly sample (with replacement) a delay from the distribution oft2−t1in our Trimmed Sample. The distribution of the delay is therefore identical between the event and placebo samples. Figure3shows that trading volume during the private window is significantly higher than the benchmark in terms of dollar value of trades, and significant for most of the delay in terms of number of trades. These results corroborate the finding of Rogers, Skinner, and Zechman (2017) that the recipients of the leaked filings enjoyed a potential trading advantage during the period we study.
Trading volume during the private window. Panel A: Number of trades. Panel B: Trades in dollar volume. This figure plots a kernel regression of the number and dollar value of trades in the Trimmed Sample at the second level during the FTP-public delay, as reported in the TAQ trade data. The regression adopts the Gaussian kernel with a bandwidth of 11.33 and 15.35 in Panels A and B, respectively. The dotted lines represent 95% confidence intervals based on local polynomial approximations. The flat “Benchmark” line shows the average number of trades per second during March 2015, the placebo period after the PDS gap was fixed. That is, for each filing in March 2015, we sample a “pseudo delay” drawn from the distribution of the timing and length of delays in the Trimmed Sample. We then obtain the number and dollar value of trades over this pseudo time window and plot the unconditional average number and dollar value of trades per second. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]
We next examine the determinants ofDelay. We find that the market condition variables, as captured by trading or filing activities or the hour of day, are the most important factors. TableIIreports regressions in whichDelay(in seconds) is the dependent variable. Columns (1) and (2) analyze the Trimmed Sample while columns (3) and (4) the “nonzero price change” subsample, that is,|Pt3−Pt1|>0. Columns (2) and (4) employ hourly fixed effects. In the Trimmed Sample, hour of day alone explains an incremental of 5% of the variation inDelay, while all other factors combined explain 14.1%. Column (2) shows that, conditional on the time of day and with the exception of market capitalization, firm characteristics—idiosyncratic volatility and share turnover—are not related to the length of the private window in any meaningful way. When a stock's market capitalization rises by one decile, the expected delay increases by about 1.2 seconds. Our best explanation for this relationship is that large firms are more likely to file in clusters (e.g., via professionals such as law firms) during busy server times. Nevertheless, this effect is modest relative to the variation inDelay. File types do not matter either after hour of day effects are controlled for. However, market conditions seem to matter. More trading (as proxied by total market trading volume over the hour) and filing (as proxied by the number of SEC filings during the last 20 minutes) are both positively related toDelay, presumably because Internet traffic is directly related to filings and is also positively correlated with market trading activities. However, these factors are exogenous to individual firms and, more importantly, to the speculators.
To summarize, the FTP-public delay, conditional on the time of day of the filing, appears to be largely random. However, the early-informed investors, that is, recipients of the leaked reports, can form some expectations about the length of the private window they will enjoy. In analyses below, we use the model in column (2) of TableIIto construct the predicted delay as our proxy forExpecteddelay.
article.II. A Model of Informed Trading with Ran:
In this section, we construct a parsimonious model in order to provide a unified structure for our empirical tests.
Our model builds on Caldentey and Stacchetti (2010), who study strategic trading by an insider in a security whose value is publicly revealed at a random time, but we modify the setting in several ways to fit our context more closely. To start, we assume that the fundamental value of a security,V, is drawn from a normal distributionN(V¯0,Ω0). Following the literature, we call speculators who have access to prematurely leaked SEC filings “insiders.” In addition, we normalize the private window to [0, τ] where the insiders receive the filing at time 0, and τ is a random stopping time triggered by the public posting of the filing that follows an exponential distribution with a hazard rate of μ. We assume that insiders are perfectly informed about μ, that is, insiders know the factors (notably time of day) that affect the length of the delay when receiving a leaked report and, presuming they have knowledge of the publicly observable predictors in TableII, can form an expectation about the delay.
Without loss of generality, we assume that an insider can estimateV, the true value of the security, following a two-step process. First, upon receiving the leaked report att=0, the insider immediately obtains a lumpy signal that is partially revealing. Second, by investing time in processing the report, the insider gradually improves her knowledge, obtaining an estimate of the security's value that asymptotes toward the true value over time. Our setup reflects the fact that a trader can often “glance” at the report (either with human eyes or machine learning) to immediately determine whether the news is positive or negative and with more time can process the signals contained in the report to refine her estimate. With our model it is straightforward to consider the special setting in which all information in the leaked report can be processed instantly att=0, but the “gradual learning” model yields more nuanced predictions as to how trading activities evolve over time.
The filtration associated with the evolution of the insider's information is denoted byFI=(FtI)t≥0. It is worth noting that the interpretation of the information flow in our setting differs from that in Caldentey and Stacchetti (2010) to better fit the setting we study. In Caldentey and Stacchetti (2010), the fundamental value of the asset evolves over time following a Martingale process and the insider perfectly observes the value of the asset at any point in time. Because we are analyzing a relatively short time window (mostly a few minutes), we assume that the true value of the asset is fixed atVbut may or may not be instantly observable by the insider. From the insider's perspective, her best subjective estimation follows an arithmetic Brownian motion.11
Other features of our model follow from, or are analogous to, those of Caldentey and Stacchetti (2010). Specifically, the position or the cumulative trade of the insider at timetis denoted byXt. Moreover, we assume that the aggregate demand process for liquidity traders follows a Brownian motionYt=σy(t)Bty, whereBtyis independent ofBtv. Also, at each timet≥0, the market maker sets a pricePtgiven his informationFtM, which consists of the full history of the price movements as well as the total order flowZt=Xt+Yt. We naturally assume that the price process is adapted to the market maker's filtrationFM=(FtM)t≥0. Finally, a strategy profile is defined as a pair(X,P), which consists of the insider's trading strategyXtand the market maker's pricing decisionPt.
Given the pricing ruleP, the trading ruleXis optimal if it maximizes the insider's payoff, given by3. The equilibrium is thus defined as a pair of(X,P)such that the trading ruleXis optimal givenPand the pricing rulePis rational givenX.
Equation7reflects the intuition that the precision of the insider's knowledge about the true value at any timetis the cumulative precision of the gradual signals plus the precision of the initial signal. This allows us to analyze the dynamics of the remaining uncertainty in the insider's estimation based on the amount of information she has received so far and the amount that is left to be revealed.
At each point in time, the total uncertainty of the market maker consists of two components: The uncertainty the insider has about the true value of the asset, and the uncertainty the market maker has about the insider's best estimation of the value. More formally, define∑t=E[(V¯t−V̂t)2|FtM]as the variance of the market maker's estimation error of the insider's valuation at timet≥0, andΩt=E[(V¯t−V)2|FtM]as the time-tuncertainty of the market marker about the true value. It follows by variance decomposition thatΩt=∑t+Γt. Next, by focusing on the pricing and learning decision of the market maker, we can see how the composition of total uncertainty evolves over time.
We assume thatβtandλtare continuous on [0, ∞), which represent the trading intensity in response to the expected price-value gap and the price impact of incremental order flow, respectively. The inverse of λ proxies for market depth. Furthermore, we require thatλtbe differentiable on (0, ∞). Proposition1characterizes the equilibrium.
Proposition 1.IfΓt<Ω0e−2μt,13there exists a continuous-time Markovian equilibrium where



Proof.See Internet Appendix SectionI.A.□

It is worth noting that in our setting,Γtis the amount of uncertainty in the true value that remains to be resolved by the insider as she receives additional signals going forward. In comparison, the insider in Caldentey and Stacchetti (2010) observes the true value in real time, but faces uncertainty about the ultimate asset value. The dynamically evolving fundamental value of the asset in Caldentey and Stacchetti (2010),Vt, plays a similar role toV̂t, the insider's best prediction of the true value, in our setup. Proposition1leads to several testable implications.
Prediction 1.Trading intensity,βt, is increasing in μ, and decreasing in the length of the expected delay.


Proof.See Internet Appendix SectionI.B.□

Intuitively, with a higher hazard rate to the expiration of private information, the insider runs a higher risk of losing her information advantage and hence trades more aggressively on her information at any given time. Before deriving other implications of Proposition1, it is useful to denote byΞt=ΣtΩtthe insider's relative information advantage over the market, recalling that∑tandΩtare the variance in the market maker's estimation error of the insider's valuation and of the true value, respectively, att≥0. This ratio can also be thought of as measuring the market maker's information disadvantage relative to the insider, given the market maker's total uncertainty about the asset's true valuation. The standard model of Kyle (1985) corresponds to the extreme case in which the insider observes the true value perfectly at each point in time, that is,Γt≡0, and hence the ratio is equal to one.
The above equation characterizes how the insider tries to camouflage her information among noise trades. The effect of noise in order flow,σy2, remains the same as in the Kyle (1985) model: Additional noise trades make it easier for the insider to conceal the intention of her trades, the insider will be more eager to trade. The relation between total uncertainty at timet,Ωt, and the insider's trading intensity is more nuanced however, as one should account for the market maker's response to the informed trades. The reason is that the market maker infers the value of the asset from order flow, as two sources of noise keep her from perfectly observing the true value of the asset. The first source of noise is the amount of uninformative trade, which the market maker cannot distinguish from the informative portion of the trade. This source of noise is standard in all variations of the Kyle (1985) model. The second source of noise, and a defining feature of the “gradual learning” in our model, comes from informed trades themselves, as the insider also does not perfectly observe the true asset value but instead places orders based on her best estimation. In equilibrium, therefore, the market maker assigns greater weight to times when she believes the insider's orders are likely to be driven by a less noisy signal, that is, the insider's orders are more informative. This also makes the insider to reduce the intensity of her trading as the strength of her private signal increases. This equilibrium leads to the somewhat counterintuitive result that an insider trades less aggressively on her private information as her relative information advantage increases, conditional on the level of noise trading.
Prediction 2.Trading intensity,βt, is increasing in noise trading,σy2, and decreasing in the insider's information advantage relative to the market,Ξt.

Based on the discussion above, we can now see why the relation between trading intensity,βt, and time,t, is ambiguous in our model as opposed to monotonically positive as in Kyle (1985). In our model, the ambiguity comes from the rate of change in the relative information advantage,∂∂tΞt. In particular, trading intensity could decrease over time if∂∂tΞtis positive and large enough. To elaborate, one may rewrite12asβ̇β=μ−Ξ̇Ξ, where the dot above the variable denotes the first-order derivative following the standard convention. Because the second term on the right-hand side is absent in models with instantaneous, full learning,βtis an unambiguously increasing function oftin these models. However, because we accommodate gradual learning, the evolution of trading intensity over time is ambiguous as it depends on the evolution of the insider's information advantage—if it grows fast enough, trading intensity,βt, may decline over time.14
Proposition1also allows us to characterize the dynamics of market depth. From10we see that market depth, the inverse ofλt, now becomes time-dependent—a new property relative to the Kyle (1985) model. Consistent with Kyle (1985),λtdepends on the total uncertainty of the market maker about the true value (Ω0) and the volume of noise trades (σy2). New to our model, however, is the feature thatλtis a decreasing function of the time that has elapsed since the initial leakage and is nonmonotonically related to the expected arrival rate of public announcement, μ.
The fact thatλtis time-varying as opposed to constant in the Kyle (1985) model is not a product of gradual learning but rather an artifact of a random stopping time. More specifically,λtincreases with μ whent<121μand vice versa. Note that1μis equal to the expected delay,τ¯. Hence, market liquidity increases (decreases) with the expected delay during the time window of[0,τ¯/2]([τ¯/2,τ]). The intuition for the nonmonotonicity is as follows. With a fixed amount of total uncertainty, the speed of information dissemination is naturally higher for the cases in which the half-life of the private information is shorter. But since the uncertainty must resolve in the limit for any rate of decay, the speed of dissemination for smaller rates of decay must eventually dominate those of larger rates of decay. This implies that at some point in time, the sensitivity of the market depth (as proxied by the inverse ofλt) to the rate of decay must change sign. On the other hand, the fact that the relation between market depth and expected delay changes sign atτ¯2is an artifact of the exponential hazard model: in this special case, the mean duration of the uncertainty is, in fact,τ¯2.
Equation11in Proposition1indicates that the rate at which the true value is revealed by the market maker,1−ΩtΩ0, depends only on the expected arrival rate of the deadline (public announcement), μ – a high hazard rate (or shorter expected delay) is associated with faster information dissemination. In contrast, the rate of information dissemination depends neither on the initial uncertainty Ω0nor the market depthλt, which echoes Kyle (1985). Empirically, information dissemination is usually measured by price convergence to value. Hence, in this section we analyze price dynamics in more depth.
As the early-informed strategically trade based on their private information and the market maker reacts strategically to these trades, the price of the asset continually converges to its true value. Due to the normal distribution of the model, conditional on the initial priceP0and the final value of the assetV, the expected level of the price at timet,Pt, is a weighted average of the two, with weights proportional to the amount of precision of the market maker's information att. In particular, the dependence of the expected price on the initial price decreases exponentially at the rate at which uncertainty resolves. This result is characterized in the following proposition.
Proposition 2.In an equilibrium of the type described in Proposition1, the dynamics of the expected price at each timetare given as



Proof.See Internet Appendix SectionI.C.□


Prediction 3.Information dissemination, or price convergence, follows


Equation15indicates that the information dissemination ratio as captured by price convergence is a simple function of the elapsed time and expected delay. More specifically, the expected rate of price convergence at each point in time relative to the initial gap is increasing in the elapsed time and decreasing in the expected delay. Notably, this relation does not depend on the initial uncertainty Ω0or the market depthλt. This result echoes11in Proposition1.
article.III. Empirical Tests:
The data set described in SectionIallows us to test the predictions derived in SectionII. For additional variables, we obtain firm-level data are from CRSP and trade-level data are from TAQ, both accessible from Wharton Research Data Services (WRDS).
We first test Predictions1and2using a panel of trading and price data at the security (i)-second (t) level within the private window. We construct the following proxies for the variables in the predictions.
Trades: Various measures of trades by the speculator on the filings for securityiduring secondt. These measures are proxies forXt(andβt) in our model. We consider both total trades and directional trades. Total trades consolidate buys and sells without signing them. Directional trades are trades that are in the “right” direction in secondtgiven the sign of the current price relative to the liquidation value (proxied byPt3orPt1+15min). Specifically,Directionalbuy=∑j=1ni,t(xi,j,t>0)·(Vi−Pi,t>0), wherej=1,…,ni,tis an index for trades belonging to thei−tpair;Directionalsellis analogously defined. Both buy- and sell-initiated trades are classified by the Lee and Ready (1991) algorithm.15Finally, we employTradesper1%price−valuegap, the ratio of trades and100∗|Pt1+15min−Pt|/Pt. Following the literature, we examine both the dollar value of trades (Panel A of TableIII) and the number of trades (Panel B) for each proxy. This results in eight proxies for the intensity of trading.
Expecteddelay: The predicted delay for the filings for securityiusing the full sample with hourly fixed effects as shown in column (2) of TableII. This measure is a proxy for the inverse of the hazard rate μ in our model. Its estimation is described in SectionI.C.
Unexpecteddelay: The difference between the actual and predicted delay for the filings for securityi. Because of its unexpected nature, it should not be used strategically by insiders. We include this variable in the regression as a placebo test.
Timesincet1: The difference between the current timetandt1. This measure corresponds totin the model.
Absolutedeviationfromvalue: Calculated as100∗|Pt1+15min−Pt|/Pt, or the percentage absolute difference between the stock price att1+15minutes, and the price at the beginning of thet-th second. This measure corresponds to|V¯−Pt|in the model, and approximates|V̂−Pt|since we do not observe the insider's valuation.
Deviationfromvalue: Calculated as100∗(Pt1+15min−Pt)/Pt, or the percentage signed difference between the stock price att1+15minutes and the price at the beginning of thet-thsecond. The signed difference is used when directional trades (buy or sell in the direction of mispricing) are analyzed.
Idiocyncraticvol: Idiosyncratic volatility of the stock during the previous 12 months. This measure is a proxy for Ω0in the model.
Shareturnover: Average daily share turnover rate (in percentage points) during the previous 12 months. This measure is a proxy forσyin the model.
Spread: The bid-ask spread (in percentage points), recorded at the beginning of each second. In some regressions, this measure serves as a control for the real-time trading liquidity of the security, which is related to λ in the model.
With respect to the dependent variables, we note thatTradesis not a direct proxy for trading intensity (β in the model), but rather is a function of both trading intensity and the price-value gap. We therefore control for|V−P|orV−Pin each regression so that the estimated coefficients reflect the determinants of trading intensity.16In addition, we control for firm size (Marketcapitalizationdecile) in each regression.
Because the key independent variable,Expecteddelay, is driven by time (hour) of the day, its effect may be confounded by varying market conditions at different times of the day. We thus add the following variables to each regression to control for general market conditions in real time as much as possible: the market-wide trading volume during the hour in whicht1belongs (in log billion dollars), the minute-level return volatility (in basis points) of SPY during the hour, the number of SEC filings uploaded during the 20-minute window prior tot1,17and an after-market dummy variable. Finally, in some regressions we further include dummy variables for Form 4, Form 8-K, and Schedule 13D filing types.
TableIIIreports regression results relatingTradesto the variables above. We focus on Panel A, which is based on the Trimmed Sample and analyzes intensity of trades in dollar terms (in units of $1, 000). Results based on number of trades are unchanged. We conduct a robustness check using the subsample with nonzero price changes during[t1,t1+15min]in Internet Appendix TableIA.II. The results are again qualitatively similar.
The first column of TableIII, Panel A, runs a tobit regression of the dollar value of all trades on the set of regressors listed above. The second column repeats the analysis after addingSpread. Because the spread, related toλtin the model, is jointly determined with trading, we include this variable as an additional control in this specification as a sensitivity check. Columns (3) and (4) analyze directional trades, that is, buy (sell) trades when the current price is below (above) the terminal value. While all trades (unsigned) are related to the absolute difference between the current price and the terminal value, directional trades are related to the signed difference. Finally, because our theoretical model suggests that the number of trades is proportional to the price divergence from the insider's estimate of value (which is not observable), column (5) uses its approximation,Trades per1%price−valuegap, as the dependent variable.
Prediction1, which states that trading intensity is decreasing in the expected delay (the inverse of μ), is well supported in all specifications. Every 10-second increase in expected delay is associated with a decrease in total trading of approximately $750, a decrease in directional trading of $2,260 to $2,760, or $64 less trading for a 1% price-value gap during a given second. The significantly larger effect using the directional trading proxies suggests that they capture informed trading more accurately.18All effects are significant at the 1% level. In contrast, the magnitude of the coefficients onUnexpecteddelayare an order of magnitude smaller and not significant. TheF−testscomparing the two coefficients indicate that they are different at less than the 1% significance level.
The other regression results are broadly consistent with the standard predictions of insider trading models, including the one studied in this paper. All specifications indicate that trading quantity is positively related to price-value divergence, both for total trades as well as directional buy and sell trades. For a 1% increase in the price-value gap, total trades increase by about $8,000 in a given second. If price is one additional percentage point below (above) the terminal value, then buy (sell) trades increase by $84,000 ($69,000). All effects are significant at the 1% level. Trades are positively and significantly (at the 1% level) related to proxies for the volume of noise trading and trading liquidity,Shareturnover,Marketcapdecile, andSpread, in line with Prediction2. However, the empirical relation between trades and our proxy for ex ante uncertainty,Idiosyncracticvol, is mixed: The relation is negative (but not significant) if we use signed trades (directional buy or sell), but positive for all trades (sum of buy and sell).
Empirically, we find that the relation between trading and time elapsed is largely neutral, both economically and statistically. Note that the “gradual learning” model predicts that trading intensity can be either increasing or decreasing with time elapsed.19However, when we consider different filing types separately (see Internet Appendix TableIA.III), we see that trades tend to decrease over time for Form 4 and Schedule 13D, from which information is relatively straightforward to extract, while the opposite is true for Form 8-K, where trades continue to be driven by new information via gradual learning. Similarly, Form 8-K and Schedule 13D are associated with significantly higher trading volume relative to Form 4 and other filings. Thus, difference in trading motive is consistent with the differences in the average information value of these forms as shown in TableI.
The relations between trades and market conditions are as expected. In particular, individual securities trading comoves with that of the market, dropping significantly during aftermarket trading, and high volatility is associated with sparse trading. Interestingly, trading volume in a given stock tends to be positively correlated with the number of filings (by all firms) during the last 20 minutes, which indicates that in this setting speculators do not face a binding constraint in allocating their attention across different filings.20
We conduct three sets of robustness tests. First, we reconstructExpecteddelayusing time fixed effects at the half-hour level to capture finer variations in delay due to the time of day, especially during the high delay period in the late afternoon. Second, we exclude observations in the last quarter-hour before the market close to filter out the effect of any rush to trade toward the end of the trading day. Third, we repeat the analysis on the subsample of filings based on a more aggressive trimming at a 331-second delay (or the 85th percentile from our Initial Sample, as compared to the 90th percentile cutoff adopted for the default Trimmed Sample). The results, which are qualitatively similar, are reported in Internet Appendix TablesIA.IVtoIA.VI.
Because we do not know the traders' identities, it is not possible to estimate the total trading profits of the early informed. However, we can estimate a kind of upper bound on the profitability of the early informed. Specifically, if we assume that all “directional trades” in this section are conducted by the early informed, and if we further assume that these positions unwind at the “liquidation value,” orPt1+15min, then the total potential profit is $6.5 million over a time period slightly shorter than four months. Note that, while we characterize this as an upper bound on profits to trading during the private window, it is important to remember that the liquidation value ofPt1+15minis not necessarily the true fundamental value of the firm, and insiders may make greater profits by waiting to sell later on. Those additional profits, however, are also available to the nonprivately informed.
In TableIVwe assess trading intensity by examining the time to the first trade in the cross section.21Here the dependent variables are the time (in number of seconds) to the first trade (the first four columns), and the time to the first “directional” trade (the last four columns), that is, the first trade that is a buy (sell) whenV=Pt1+15min>Pt(V<Pt). If we assume that a privately informed trader is able to predict the sign of price-value divergence and that the Lee and Ready (1991) algorithm classifies orders into buy- or sell-initiated significantly above chance, then the second definition captures informed trading more closely. However, the first definition is a more robust definition of potentially informed trading. Both dependent variables are always censored at zero andt2(or the end of the private window, if no trading occurs during the private window), so we employ censored regression analysis (i.e., a two-sided tobit model).
Consistent with TableIII, the results in TableIVsupport the view that insiders are in less of a rush to trade when they anticipate a longer delay. Specifically, for every 100-second increase in expected delay, the time to the first trade increases by 55 to 91 seconds.22The coefficients onUnexpecteddelayalso tend to be significant. These results could be due to insiders having a more refined model of the expected delay than we have, or could be driven by the censored observations (no trade during the window) where longer actual delays are associated with a higher lower bound on the time to first trade. It is important to note thatF-tests for the differences between the coefficients on expected and unexpected delays reject equality at the 1% level in 14 out of 16 specifications.
The coefficients on the covariates are also intuitive and consistent with the corresponding coefficients in TableIII. Overall, more trading liquidity and noise trading (proxied byShareturnover,Marketcapdecile, andSpread) lead to more trading, as does higher information value of the filing (proxied byIdiosyncraticvoland|AR(t1,t1+15minutes)|). While|AR(t1,t1+15minutes)|is an ex post outcome that is affected by trading, we present additional evidence suggesting that this variable is potentially endogenous. Across all filing types, Schedule 13D, the type that allows for rapid information processing (as it simply reveals the presence of an activist blockholder) and contains a high level of information on average (see TableI, Panel B), generates the fastest first trades—about 20 to 50 seconds faster than Forms 4 and 8-K. Thus traders' attention does not seem to be diverted by recent filings, but rather they trade faster when more filings are coming out, consistent with TableIII.
Standard microstructure theory assumes that speculators use market orders to take advantage of their private information. However, empirical studies (notably, Kaniel and Liu (2006) and Collin-Dufresne and Fos (2015)) show that the privately informed may trade in such a way that limit orders are more informative than market orders. To test the choice of limit versus market orders, we classify all trades during the private window into limit or market orders following the same methodology as in Collin-Dufresne and Fos (2015).23We then run an unordered multinomial logit model to predict the use of limit and market orders (relative to the base state of no trade during each filing-second). If there are both market and limit order trades in a security-second, we code the order type with higher trading volume. The results are reported in TableV.
We find that a longer expected delay favors limit orders but only whenSpreadis taken into account. This is intuitive in that limit orders take longer to execute than market orders and therefore are a luxury to be used in cases involving a long delay. Limit orders exhibit similar sensitivity as market orders to the divergence of price from value (proxied by|V−P|) at each point in time. However, in Internet Appendix TableIA.VIIIwe show that among the subsample of filings that are accompanied by price changes during[t1,t3], wider price-value divergence is significantly (at the 10% level) more likely to generate limit orders than market orders, which is broadly consistent with Kaniel and Liu (2006) and Collin-Dufresne and Fos (2015). Finally, the choice between the two types of orders depends on general market conditions, as indicated by aggregate market trading volume, market-wide volatility, and aftermarket status.
We now test Prediction3about the dynamics of information dissemination. Prediction3models the expected price change fromt0at a given time relative to the initial value-price gap,|V−P0|. The empirical tests rely on the observed pricePtinstead. Moreover, because the proxy for the initial value-price gap,Pt3−Pt1, is zero for most of the observations in our sample, we rescale the price change withPt1instead.24The empirical analog to the ratio of information dissemination thus becomes|Pt−Pt1|Pt1ifPtlies in the same direction fromPt1asPt3, or(Pt−Pt1)∗(Pt3−Pt1)>0(wheret3=t1+15min). It is zero otherwise, that is, if the price moves in the wrong direction, the amount of information dissemination is taken to be zero. We use a tobit model to accommodate the censoring. TableVIreports the regression results. In Internet Appendix TableIA.IX, we conduct a robustness check in which “negative information dissemination” is not censored.
Consistent with Prediction3, the rate of information dissemination is slower when insiders enjoy a longer expected delay. Specifically, for a 100-second increase in expected delay, the rate of information dissemination (or the convergence of the price-value gap) is about 8 to 14 basis points lower, significant at the 1% level. Also consistent with Prediction3, information dissemination progresses with time: for every 100 seconds sincet1, the price converges to value by 6 to 7 basis points. The qualitative inferences are similar in the uncensored version in Internet Appendix TableIA.IX.
Although the model predicts that the speed of price dissemination should bear no relation to ex ante uncertainty, Ω0, the coefficient on its proxy,Idiosyncraticvolwhen included is significantly positively associated with the pace of information dissemination.25Moreover, price convergence is faster when market-wide trading activity is high and is slower during the aftermarket. Finally, Schedule 13D enjoys the fastest rate of information dissemination, consistent with results in TablesIIIandIVthat trades on this type of disclosure are the most forthcoming and front loaded.
Our model predicts that inverse market depth, as captured byλt, is nonmonotonic in the expected delay. However, as discussed in SectionII.B, this is an artifact of the random stopping time with constant hazard, not of gradual learning. Empirically, we are not able to consistently estimate such a nonmonotonic relation or to provide a clear test for market depth as the outcome of insider's strategic trading. In TableIA.Xin the Internet Appendix we report results on the determinants of trading liquidity at the security-second level, where the dependent variables are the spread and the absolute price change. While expected delay is positively associated with the spread, it is negatively associated with the absolute price change, where the relation could conflate the effects of market depth and information dissemination. Other factors, such as shocks to uninformed volume, may also affect the relation between liquidity, price volatility, and trading volume (Collin-Dufresne and Fos (2016)).
article.IV. Conclusion:
Using data from an unusual episode in which SEC filings that contained market-moving information were disseminated to a small group of investors before the public release of that information, we examine a quasi-natural experiment that allows us to test insider trading strategies against a random deadline and the process through which private information is impounded into stock prices. The study serves as a counterpart to Koudijs's (2015a) analysis of insider trading during the 18th century, with the benefit of the more granular trading information afforded by modern data to help reveal the process through which private information is incorporated into stock prices. Our results are consistent with the hypotheses that trading intensity and the pace at which prices incorporate information decrease with the expected delay until public release, while noise trading and relative information advantage play similar roles as in standard microstructure theories assuming a fixed time window.
Editors: Stefan Nagel, Philip Bond, Amit Seru, and Wei Xiong
